rationale_for_baseline_tier: Severe neuroglycopenic hypoglycaemia — the patient is confused, staring, drowsy and barely rousable, cannot follow commands and cannot swallow safely, with a bedside glucose of 2.1 mmol/L and no effective glucose delivered yet. This is an immediate, airway-threatening, life-threatening emergency, and a patient-reportable time-critical feature (altered consciousness / inability to swallow) is present, so T5 (call 000 now) is the only appropriate tier. Recovery positioning and nil-by-mouth are the interim measures, plus a glucagon rescue kit if one is available; the ambulance provides airway protection, IV glucose and observation. The recurrent unprovoked lows then need urgent endocrine work-up, but that does not downgrade the acute 000 response.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Confusion/drowsiness with inability to swallow, or seizure or collapse, in an acutely hypoglycaemic person
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call 000 immediately; lay him on his side, give nothing by mouth; if an in-date glucagon kit is available and a carer able, give it now into the outer thigh
escalation_edges.rationale: Neuroglycopenia with an unprotected airway can progress to seizure, aspiration and coma within minutes; only emergency services plus parenteral/rescue glucose can reverse it safely.
escalation_edges.communication_template: He's too drowsy to swallow safely — call 000 now and tell them he's confused with what looks like a severe low blood sugar. Don't put anything in his mouth. Lay him on his side. If by any chance you have a glucagon emergency kit and it's in date, give the injection into the outside of his thigh while you wait.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: After recovery, any return of confusion, sweating or weakness, or a home glucose reading below 4.0 mmol/L, especially with fasting or exercise
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Give 15–20 g fast-acting carbohydrate if he can swallow and recheck in 10–15 minutes; if he cannot swallow or does not improve, call 000
escalation_edges.rationale: These unprovoked lows recur unpredictably until the source of excess insulin is found; a recurrence with impaired swallowing is again a 000 emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Even after he comes round, these lows can come back — especially if he goes without food or exercises. If he gets confused, sweaty or weak again, or a meter reads under 4.0, give something sugary if he can swallow and recheck; if he can't swallow or doesn't pick up, call 000.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: He wakes fully and can swallow safely
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Give fast-acting then long-acting carbohydrate, keep monitoring, and arrange urgent endocrine referral / same-day medical review — do not simply let him sleep it off
escalation_edges.rationale: Once conscious and able to swallow, oral carbohydrate and monitoring apply, but an unprovoked severe hypoglycaemic event mandates urgent specialist work-up; this does not downgrade the acute 000 response for the current emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Once he's fully awake and can swallow, give him juice and then a proper snack, keep an eye on him, and get him seen urgently today — this needs specialist testing to find why his sugar dropped, not left until it happens again.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: The patient is obtunded and fully dependent; a single housemate is the only person present and must both act and stay on the line to 000.
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give clear, single-step instructions: call 000 first (or put it on speaker), nil by mouth, recovery position. A non-diabetic household almost certainly has no glucagon kit, so 000 and airway positioning are the only levers until paramedics arrive.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Recurrent unprovoked fasting/exertional hypoglycaemia in a non-diabetic (suspected insulinoma) — the lows will keep recurring until the cause is localised and treated.
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Emphasise urgent endocrine referral and, in the interim, avoiding fasting and intense exercise and keeping carbohydrate to hand, because further severe episodes are likely before definitive treatment.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance right now and say he is confused and barely responding with what looks like a severe low blood sugar. Do not give him anything to eat or drink while he cannot swallow. Roll him onto his side so his airway stays clear. If you happen to have a glucagon emergency injection kit and it is in date, give it into the outer thigh while you wait. Stay with him and unlock the door for the paramedics.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 is available 24 hours a day — this is an emergency and must not wait for clinic hours or for him to 'sleep it off'.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The ambulance and hospital records should go to the GP with an urgent endocrinology referral for a supervised 72-hour fast (paired glucose, insulin, C-peptide, proinsulin and a sulfonylurea/meglitinide screen) and pancreatic localisation imaging to investigate the recurrent unprovoked hypoglycaemia, plus provision of a home glucose meter and hypoglycaemia education.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating an obtunded, non-swallowing, critically hypoglycaemic patient as anything less than a 000 emergency — e.g. being reassured that he has no diabetes, advising oral sugar, home monitoring, or a morning GP visit.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Aspiration, hypoglycaemic seizure, coma, anoxic brain injury or death from delayed reversal of a rapidly fatal but treatable cause.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recognising the emergency but advising self-transport to ED rather than an ambulance, when the patient has a threatened airway and needs paramedic-level care en route.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: T5 is correct here; over-triage is not a meaningful risk in this presentation.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the acute confusion and visual disturbance could represent a primary neurological event (stroke), CNS infection or seizure rather than hypoglycaemia — and, once the low is corrected, what is driving unprovoked hypoglycaemia in a non-diabetic.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the reversible hypoglycaemia first while escalating to 000 — it is a fully reversible mimic and must be corrected regardless of diabetes status; the recurrent unprovoked lows are then worked up urgently by endocrinology (supervised fast, insulin/C-peptide, imaging), not diagnosed remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Being reassured by the absence of diabetes, activating a stroke/CNS-infection pathway or giving a sedative/anticonvulsant before correcting the glucose, or withholding rescue while deliberating the cause.
